speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When we finished the last reel, you were talking about coming home and finding out--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That your was gone and that you realized--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't figure out why they didn't take your brother, Joshua.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, Joshua, right. So, they were talking about hiding, starting to hide, so that-- because they were taking transports every day from [? Shodula ?] to Auschwitz. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that's where the transports were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I was-- I, I, I knew. I even knew where they took my mother with this, you know, because my father immediately said that's where they took her, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you came to Sosnowiec, and, and you talked a little bit about the fact that you knew that they were-- that something was being built out at the army barracks. Were you aware what was being built out there while you were still in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were still in Oswiecim, rather?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was not aware of, of any kind of construction going on in, in what used to be a, a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks for a-- for the Polish army, yes, camp, Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: But once you were in Sosnowiec, how did you come to find out that people-- when the-- when people were taken, that that's where they went? How did you know this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we-- that's all they talked about, you know, that they are sending the people to Auschwitz, to-- and the old people are being gassed. And, and it was a-- like an open secret, yeah, in [? Shodula, ?] you know, that-- and they, and some people are sent to, to work. And some people are sent to the gas chamber. And so [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father wanted to find a place for hiding, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: For hiding, yes. So what they did is, is they, they closed off the, the, the normal entries to the basement. And they, they cut a hole in, in, in the floor in one of the apartments, and, and put a ladder down. And then the-- and on top of the, the opening, go through to the-- opening to the basement, they nailed a, a table and a carpet. And every time you open it up, you move the table away to go down there, and then-- and a chair. And then you moved it back so it looked like that there is nothing below.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when it got to the point that we-- that the time came to hide, because we were concerned, you know? We-- as I told you before, the-- depends on the-- they were always scattering people. So with my father's background, they, they-- you know, depends who you talk to, and, and what kind of a person. They would say, [GERMAN SPEECH]? Or they would say, [GERMAN SPEECH]. You-- excuse my expression-- shitty Jew and, and give him a few [INAUDIBLE] on, on his face. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so he, he realized that the end is, is near to, you know, where they would liquidate [? Shodula. ?] And that was a big concern. So we. We went down this place in the basement. There was a whole apparatus, as I said. They cut a hole which fit right back in, in, in. And on top of the hole, they put a-- like a rug, which they nailed to it. And then they nailed a table to it. So when they moved it, there was, there is no way to realize that there's a opening below.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was a totally, total fiasco. Because they took-- first of all, there was no sanitary facilities. There was no bathrooms. There was no water. They took down some water. It was-- the smell was horrible. And there was no air coming in. Because they were not smart enough to realize to put in a vent somewhere. And maybe they were, however, then they probably were afraid that the Germans would listen to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they, they, so we went down there. And I-- we were down there maybe a day or two, maybe three days. I don't remember exactly how long. But I told my father, I said, I am not-- I don't want to be here. I said, this-- when they find that we are here, hiding, they will throw in one or two grenades and kill everyone there. And, and I don't want to die this way, I told him. So we were-- and so we-- one evening at-- when it got-- when became dark, we walked from [? Shodula ?]-- my father, myself, and my younger brother. And he must have-- my father must have gotten some maps to, to realize which way to, to go to another camp, another ghetto, which was from Bielsko, not far from Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked-- excuse me-- we walked all night and through the fields, through the cornfields, and through-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] off the beaten path. And off, off-- so, so there's no roads. And no one could-- and we came there, dead tired, to this place outside of Bielsko thatt was also a, a ghetto, and where my aunt and uncle and, and, and also the youngest brother, and, and, and, and two brothers. One was-- one's name is Jack Rosenbaum, who is still alive, his brother, Shemek, who was about the same age as my brother and, and my aunt and uncle. And they dug a hole in, in-- outside, in a, in a storage bin. And, and they put a mattress down in the hole. And they also put wood on top of it, you know, and so that the Germans couldn't-- and so when we came there, was no room for us. It was like a-- real like a grave, that they slept in, enough room for, you know, sideways to lie, for four people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we couldn't get down there. So we were hiding in the same storage bin. And there was a lot of wood, firewood. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were there maybe a day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the German, the-- I don't know whether it was the Wehrmacht or the SS group, which at that time it was totally confusing. They, they said, we know you are there. And we're going count to three. And if you don't come out, we will throw in a grenade.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father immediately said, come on, boys. And he went out and raised his arms. He says, [GERMAN SPEECH]. Don't shoot. We are coming. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and so then they took us to a collecting place.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know what exactly happened at the same time to my cousin Jack and, and his, his father, mother, and the, and the-- his younger brother. But maybe they just remained there for a while, maybe same day, maybe the next day. And-- because they were satisfied that we came out. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we, we were taken to this collecting place, where they put us on a cattle train [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and took us Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the cattle train like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a, it was a train that had a roof with-- but it had little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] openings, you know, for the cattle to be able to breathe. And, you know, they-- when they open the-- I mean, when they lock the door, you get such a click, you know? And that-- I felt like the click in my heart, because I knew where we are going. It, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was very chaotic. There were kids, babies, on the floor, my father, myself in the train-- mothers, pregnant women, [CRYING] old people, young people, people my age, people my brother's age. I don't know exactly how long we were on the train. But I remember that the kids were crying, and there was such a chaos, so-- so I collected whatever I could-- sugar, candy or whatever. And I, I sit down. And I played with the kids. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because I knew. [CRYING] [INAUDIBLE] And-- and it got-- and it became worse. The Germans came, the SS troopers, when we arrived in Auschwitz, took these babies and threw them against the train and threw them down on the ground, stepped on their heads with their boots. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was such a chaos. And they were running around. The SS group was hitting people with the butts of their rifle, rifle butts. Dogs were barking and biting. It was like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] seemed like you just arrived in hell. And it was a picture that I will never forget. Mothers crying. They took the babies away from them. They threw them on the floor. They stepped on their heads, threw them against the car. Old people, they kicked and beat and, and hit them constantly with rifle. Blood was all over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then they was an officer there who was-- to me, it looked like a little baton, in the faces, right and left, right and left. Well, I was holding onto my brother in one hand and my father on the other side. And, and he says-- the SS trooper said, he cannot go with you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they tried to separate us. [CRYING] And then I said to my brother, don't worry. I'll, I'll come across. In Polish I told him, and I'll get you back here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was the last I saw of-- this was the last that I saw my brother, Joshua. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they marched us in through a gate where it says, 'Arbeit Macht Frei'. This was engraved on my mind. And, and I can still see it in front of my eyes. And, and we went through, took us on another barracks. And, and we had to strip completely, and took everything away. We were standing nude. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and they deloused us. They had those pumps with powder, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what they call [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] delousing, you know? So they will getted all the lice, kill them. And we, we were standing in line. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My father said-- he, he was always trying to protect me, so he was standing behind me. He, he said-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] the guy said, next, next, next. And then I step up next. And, and the guy said to him, he says, with a vulgar expression he said, you schmuck. He says, why didn't you kill him before you brought him here, to my father. And then he took my arm. And he stretched it. And he squeezed it very hard and took a tattoo-- this was like, looked like a pen. They are different dots. And he tattooed my number on. And as of that moment, my name and my life became a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you hold up-- could you hold up your arm so that [INAUDIBLE] can, can film that? And could you tell us your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: The number is engraved in my mind. It's [GERMAN SPEECH], which is 133,527 in English. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where, really, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things started to happen for the worse. Couldn't get much worse, really. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I finally got to a place where they gave you a-- like it looked like a, a military canteen, you know. And they give you some soup, which [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] something looked like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] used dishwater.

speaker: interviewee
text: And and I sat down, finally. I said, oh, [INAUDIBLE]. And I can sit down, at least. So I sit down. And I started to eat. This was in the evening-- soup. And the guy next to me, he says, you know what they're serving tonight? What kind of food it is? I said no, but I'm very hungry. He says, it's dog, dog meat. So that's lesson number one of survival. So I pushed it away. And as soon as I pushed it away, he poured it in, in his dish. This is number one lesson of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the survival of the fittest. And so I was hungry again. And I never got to eat at-- I don't know for how many days. And whatever my father and I had, we sort of shared together. And, and we-- my father always said, eat whatever you can. Because they may steal it from you, you know? His experienced, probably, in, in, in Siberia taught him quite a few things that I was not cognizant of.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] we went. We-- next morning, we got up. We got a piece of bread, and-- just a piece of bread. I don't know if they gave us anything else. And then they took us to work, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work was it? Where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: It looked like it's a-- it was a quarry we marched to. It wasn't too far away, or maybe just a, a, a storage yard of, of, looked like stones and bricks and building material, you know? So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they told you to take you jacket off, you know? Because they gave you this pajama prison uniform with the stripes, and to reverse it, and put it-- put the back in the front and the front in the back. And they-- and used to hold down. And they would pour in, or put in, you know? And you would take it from one place and put it in another place, you know, and carry it in your--

speaker: interviewer
text: Carry what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stone, mainly stone, from one place-- you know, in, in your jacket, you know, whatever-- and put it someplace else, where somebody else would take it off. Or you-- sometimes, you would just drop it and go back. And this is the kind of work I remember doing when I-- during the time I was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the quarry, while you were working in this quarry, did you witness any beatings, any murders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Constantly beating.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Murder, [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewer
text: What sorts of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shooting, rifle butts, kicking, and lot of bashing people on the head with their rifle butts. And how you constantly heard this, you [GERMAN SPEECH], you shitty Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in this-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the evening when we came back, you lined up again for food. And, and you got a, you know, with the ladle, they, they would. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you ate real quick. And I was-- first time is shame on me, you know? So I-- and my father said, eat everything. Always, he said, eat everything. Then I realized really why he said it. But I'll tell you the story later. Because he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and this is like a constant. So every day, you got up. You did the same thing. You lined up on the [INAUDIBLE], where you-- and they count. Every day, they count you so that they know how many people left, and how many piece of bread. It got to the point where the kapos would, would take-- make us carry out dead bodies, and prop them up, and hold on to them so they can get more pieces of bread, the kapos. Because they counted the people. And, and it was cold. It was-- actually, it, it seems like it was cold. Because it must've been like maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] August, I would say, roughly, and maybe because the-- you were always hungry. That didn't help, either. So then they would call, and who wants seconds? So when I saw that the people were pushing, you know, and they would-- and the kapos that would dish out the food would take the ladle and split your head with it, and the blood on the ladle. And then they dunked it in again and, and poured the next. So I realized right away that if I do that, I'll be killed. You know, I'm not going to do it. And my father said, you know, same thing. And this went on for a few weeks like that. Yes? What about the smells? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the smell was horrible. And, and the, the conditions were appall-- appalling conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- because you, you, you didn't change your clothes. You couldn't, you couldn't take a shower. You couldn't wash. You, you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lived like an animal. And they packed you in, in those [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bunks, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which were, like, maybe 10 to a bunk, you know, 10 to a bunk. And it was-- my father got very sick. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And his, his feet would started to swell. And then his head was swelling. This was usually [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the beginning of a total deterioration from-- when, when I saw this, I, I was very upset. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] one day, we went, we went to work. And they said-- because he could hardly walk at that time-- they said, all the sick people, you don't have to work today. You don't have to go to work today. So they-- they-- we marched out of the-- to the work. And he-- when I came back in the evening, he was gone. He was-- I, I saw him that morning. I said goodbye to him, told him I'll see you later. And, and I never saw-- [CRYING] never saw him again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father, [CRYING] I remember, when I was a little boy, he used to carry my younger brother and, and wait for Shabbat to be over. He used to sing to him [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I remember when I came back, [CRYING] and all these wonderful things that my father-- it's like a total void. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And all of a sudden, I didn't have anyone to, to tell me anything, to give me advice, and to protect me. And unfortunately, I couldn't protect him. And, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] and Auschwitz was such a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] horrible place. You, you-- if you, if you had to go to the bathroom, first of all, the facilities were like one barrack, they were taken. And, and there were holes, you know, where you would sit down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so when I saw what's happening, I used to try to go to the bathroom during daylight time, during working hours. Because at nighttime, some of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] guards from the towers would take potshot at you and kill people to-- just going to the bathroom. And the only thing I said, how do I get out of this hell hole? So-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] so-- We're going to change the reel, OK? We're going to stop and change the reel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Change the reel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
